Dear l,lr . FeI lows :
This is in response to your letter requesting assistance on

concerning their respons ibi l ities under the conf l ict-of-interest
provisioni of the Poiitical Reforn Act (the 'rAct") -1 since your
luestions are general in nature and you have not naned a specific
e'lectea officiil 
on whose behalf you have requested this advice,
hre are providing infornal responses to your questions.z
Please note, this letter is based on the facts presented to
us. The coDnission does not act as the finder of fact in
providing advice. (In re ooelsbv (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) In
iaaition, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate
any conduct that has already taken place.
OUESTIONS
1. Are the nenbers of the Irvine Transportation Authority
public officials subject to the disclosure and d isqual i ficat ion
provisions of the Act?

2. If the members of the frvine Transportatlon Authority are
public officlals, under uhat clrcumstances wilL they have a
conflict of lnterest wj.th respect to a governDental decision?
3. will the decisions of the Irvine Transportation Authority
affect the economic interests of the nenbers in substantially the
sane manner as the decisions will affect the public generally?
CONCLUS IONS
1. Since the Irvine Transportation Authority has decision-
naking authority with respect to contracts and the investnent of
funds collected frorn fares, fees, and tolls, it is a
decisionnaking body under the Act and its ureurbers are tipublic
officialsl subject to the disclosure and disqua I i f ication
provisions of the Act.
2. No nenber of the Irvine Transportation Authority may
rnake, participate in making or in any rray attempt to use his or
her ofiicial position to influence a governnental decision in
$rhich the nenber knoss or has reason to know he or she has a
financial interest.
3. It does not appear that the decisions of the lrvine
Transportation Authority wifl affect the public aenerally in the
sarne nanner as they nay affect the enployers of the various
nembers. consequently, we do not belleve that the industries that
appoint the uenEers are a significant 6egTl0ent of the public
generally as conteraplated by the Act.
FACTS
The Irvine Transportation Authority (the rrauthority'r) was
established in titay 1989 to advise the city council on traffic
issues. of the authority's eleven ruembers, five are Irvine City
Councilmenbers, one is a public nenber appointed by the city
council, and the rernaining five are appointed by the city council
after nomination by the following locaI organizations: (1) the
University of California at Irvine; (2) the fndustriaJ. League of
orange county,' (3) the Irvine chamber of commerce; (4) the Irvine
Business comptex consortium,' and, (5) the Building fndustry of
Southern california. 
The nominees generally are employed by
businesses which are menbers of the associations. You have not
provided information with respect to these employers.
The Irvine Code provides that -the authority does not have the
por.rer to inplenent any city policies or procedures concerning
traffic issues. The authority nakes recoromendations to the city
council, uhich the council may accept or reject. In order for a
decision by the authority to becorne operative, it nust be adopted


Our Fl■e No. ■-9■-395
Page 3
by a city council resolutLon or ordinance. You also stated that,
tristoricitly, the city council has not approved the authority's
recorumendations without significant nodification.
The authority is currently considering a proposed ordinance
which will inpose reporting, adninistrative and facilities-
irnprovernent requirenents on: (1) conpanies enploying 25 or nore
peisons in lrvine; (2) owners of buildings or conplexes that meet
or exceed a certain gross square footage thresholdi and,
(3) devetopers of new non-resldential projects.
Conpanies employing 25 or nore Persons in the city constitute
775 of the 7,459 enployers in Irvine, or 10.4t. Those 775
companj.es enploy 74t of the uorkers in Irvine. The number of
builainqs or conplexes subject to the ordinance is estimated to be
130. li is possible the proposed ordinance nay be nodified to
affect eurployers of 50 or nore, rather that 25 or more' There are
sone 406 such eraployers in Irvine (5.4t of all employers) who
enploy 61.9t of all enployees in frvine.
ANALYSIS
Pubtic Officials
The Political Reforn Act rras enacted by the people of the
State of california by initiative in 1974. The Purpose for the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act rras to
ensure that public officlals, whether elected or appointed, rrould
perforn thei; duties ln an lnpartial nanner, free frora bias caused
Ly their own econonic interests or the economic interests of
persons rrho have supPorted then. (Section 81001'(b).)
Regulation 18700(a) (1) includes rrith the definition of
"public official" the folloving:
(1) 
trMemberrr sha11 include, but not be
linited to, salaried or unsalaried nerobers of
boards or conmissions with dec ision-raaking
authority. 
A board or conrnission has decision-
naking authority whenever:
(A) It may make a final governmental
decision;
(B) It nay cornpel a governmental
decision; or it nay prevent a governrnental
decision either by reason- of an exclusive
power to initiate the decision or by reason of
a veto which nay not be overridden; or


Our File No. 
■-9■-395
Page 4
(c) It Eakes substantive recommendations
which are, and over an extended period of tiroe
have been, regrularly aPproved without
significant anendment or nodification by
another public official or governmental
agency.
According to the facts you have provided in your-letter, you
do not believt that neDbers of the lrvine Transportation Authority
have decisionrnaking authority. You atated the authority advises
the city council on traffic issues which nay only be inplenented
by city- council action. You also stated the authority cannot
c6npe1- or prevent the city council froro ultinately exercising
their independent judgment. Finalty, you have stated that the
city council has not historicatl'y aPProved the authority's
rec6nmendations without significant nodification. Under such
circumstances, the authority would not be a decisionroaking body
under the Act.
However, according to Sections vI .F-530 and vI .F-532 of the
Irvine Code Provided by your office, the authority has the express
Dower to exelute contracts trithout city consent and to nake
investrnents with funds held by the authority which have been
collected froro fares, fees, and tolls. 
Such powers could give
rise to a conflict oi interest where the authority is the ultiuate
decisionnaker and a nenber is financially interested in a
potential contractor or investrnent. Since the authority aPpears
Lo have the polrer to nake final governnental declslons, we uould
conclude that the authority is a decisionmaking body under the Act
and that the nenbers of the authority are rrpublic off icialsi'
subject to the disclosure and disqual if ication provlslons of the
Act.
conflicts of Interest
A public official is required to disclose afl his or her
econoniL interests that couLd foreseeably be affected by the
exercise of the officiat's duties. (Sections 87200-87313.) In
addition, no public official at any level of state or locaL
governnent raay make, participate in naking or in any rray attempt
€o use his or her official position to influence a governnental
decision in which the official knows or has reason to know the
official has a financial interest. (Section 87100.)
Please note that participation in governmental decisions has
been interpreted broadly in furtherance of the goals of the Act.
Thus, trhere a member is disquali;iafi with respect to authority
decisions, the member is also prohibited fron making contacts
with, appearing before, or otherwise attenpting to influence, any
nember, officer, enployee or consultant of the authority
concerning the governnental decision. (Regulations 18700 and
18700. 1. )


litoreover, Regulatlon 18700.1(c) provides that uith regard to
a governmental decision which ls before another governmental
agency, an official is attenptlng to use his or her offlcial
position to influence the decislon lf, for the purpose of
influencing the declsion, the official acts or purports to act on
behalf of, or as the reoresentative of. his or her aqencv to any
menber, officer, eDployee or consultant of an agency.
section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision uilL have a naterial financial effect,
distinguishable fron the effect on the public Aenerally, on the
officiil 
or a Denber of his or her inurediate fanily or on:
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a cornnercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating thro hundred fifty
doLlars ($ZSol or Dore in value provided to,
received by or pronised to the public official
within 12 nonths prior to the ti:ne when the
decislon is rnade.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any posltion of nanagenent.
In your letter you discussed potential conflicts of lnterest
for the urenbers of the authority due to their ernployuent nith
buslnesses that could be inpacted by the authority's declsions.
Any person or business that has nade any paynent to a nember
auiing the 12 rnonths prior to a decision is a potentially
disqu;lifying econoruic interest and the roernber is prohibited from
participating in any decision r^Ihich wiIl have a reasonably
foreseeable and material financial effect on that source of
-""";;T_
whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the tine a governnental decision is rnade
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it wiII occur. certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a nere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

87■03(d).

A financial effect on the economic interest must also be
roaterial to reguire disquali fication. The Coromission has adopted
various regulations which contain several objective standards for

depending on the nature of the decision and economic interest.
(Regulation L87o2.) For exanple, hrhere an economic interest is
dir6ctry involved in the authoriiy's decisionl as an applicant or
the subject of a decision, the rnember will generally be
disqualified fron participating in the decision. Since rre assume
ttra€ ttre ernployers were not the applicants or naured parties in the
decision of-thL authority, but will be affected as a class, it
does not appear the enployers rrould be directly affected.
However, even if the econornic interest is not directly
involved in the authority's decision, the Eenbers are still
required to disqualify themselves fro:o - participating in decisions
which rnay indirectly have a :naterial financial effect on the
economic- interest. whether the indirect effect of a decision on a
business entity is rnaterial depends on the financial size of the
business entiti. 
Regulation ta7o2.2 Provides different thresholds
of nateriality for the following:
1. Business entities listed on the New York Stock Exchange
or the American Stock Exchange, or business entities on the
Fortune Magazine Directory of the 5Oo larqest U..S. industrial

(Regulation L8702.2 (a) and (d).)

securities Dealers National I'{arket List or any business entity
with net tangible assets of at least $18,000r00o and pre-tax
incone for the last fiscal year of at least S2,500,000.
(Regulation L8702.2 (b) and (e) . )
3. Business entities not fitting the reguirenents of (a) or
(b) but which are listed on the Pacific stock Exchange or qualify
for public sale in california and are listed on the Eligible
secuiities List maintained by the California Departroent of

least S400,000. (Regulation LA702.2 (c) and (f) . )
4. For any business entity not covered in one of the
categories set forth above, Regulation L8702.2 (g) governs.

4 Ar, ."orrornic interest is directly involved in a decision when

La702.1(b).)

AssuDing that a neDber's employer ls a relatively 
sna11
business entity, Regrulation La7O2.2(g) provides that the effect of
a decision is naterial where:
(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,00o or nore; or

entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or

fiscal year ln the anount of $2,500 or Dore; or

or decrease ■n the value of
of S10,000 。r more。
result in the increase
assets or Iiabilities
You have not provided us with inforroation concerning the
financial size of the employers of the appointed nembers.
Iloreover, you have not discussed the potential financial effect of
the ordinances on the eroployers. If the effect of the ordinance
will exceed the appropriate standards in Regulation 18702.2, the
nember will have I conttict of lnterest with respect to decisions
on the ordinance. If the effect of the ordinance sill not exceed

Ue aUii to participate. we cannot deternine the nagnitude.of the
financlal eifect oi the decision on the enployers ln question; we
nust leave this deternination to you and the nembers of the
authority.
FinaLly, even if a nenber wl}l have a conflict of interest
eith respect to soroe authority decisions, souetimes a complex
series oi decisions nay be divided into separate decisions so that
a nenber rnay participate in other portions. Such a segmentation
is only appropriate where the segrnented decisions are not
interralated to the decision fron which the official is
disgualified and the official has no other disqualifying interest
with respect to the segnented decisions. (Huffaker Advice Letter,
No. A-85-343.) For example, a regulation which imposes

a member's employer nay be a decision for which the nenber is
disqualified. However, the menber nay be able to participate in
the adoption of a companion provision that affects a different
segment of the business cornmunity.
where other subsequent decisions are separable, the following
procedure should be followed to per.rnit the member to participate:
(1) The decisions for which the nenber has a disqualifying
financial interest should be segregated fron the other decisions.

(21 The decisions froB which the Eember is disqualified
should be considered first, and a final decision reached by the

(3) once a decision has been Dade on the portions of the
plan for uhich the nenber has a disgualifying interest, the rnernber
lnay participate in the deliberatlons regarding other portions of
the plan, so long as those deliberations do not result in a

official was disqualified. (Huffaker Advice Letter, suDra.)
t'PubIic Generallvrt Exception
1. Effect on the Pooulation of Irvine
Public officiaLs with financial interests that will be
ruaterially affected by a decision may still participate in the
decision if tne effect on their interests is not distinguishable
fron the effect on the public aenerally. For the 'rpublic gener-
aIly" exception to app1y, a decj.sion !0ust affect the official's
interests in substantiallv the sane manner as it woul'd affect a
siqnificant secrnent of the public. (Regulation 18703.)
The "public[ consists of the entire jurisdiction of the
agency in question. (In re Owen (L9761 2 FPPC ops. 77.) This is
s6 because all the residents of the jurisdiction are constituents
of the official. 
Thus, the authority meubers nay participate in
the various decisions coning before their agency If the effect on
the nember's financial lnterest ls @ 
as the
effect on a sionificant secrment of the population of the Clty of
Irvine .
The comnission has never adopted a strict arithnetic fornula
for deterrnining what constitutes a significant segTnent of the
public. Horrever, in order to apply the rrpublic generallyrl
exception, the affected population nust be larqe and heterodeneous
in nature. (In re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 62.) According to
your facts the affected class constitute Less than one percent of
the poputation of Irvine.
Moreover, the decision's effect on a business entity/employer
of a member would have to be substantial,ly the sane as the effect
of the decision on a significant segment of the public. You
stated that the proposed ordinance would affect only cornpanies
ernploying 25 or nore persons in the city. 
Thus, the effects of

the ordinance on the BeDber's eroployer will be distinguishable
from the effect of the decision on the public aenerally.5
2. Industries. Trade or Profession as the riPublic Generallvt'
The Irvine Code describes the purpose and coEposition of the
authority as follows:
The construction, acquisition, naintenance,
operation and iuprovenent of transportation
facilities and the provision of transportation

ind private property acquired and are rnunicipal

of Irvine granted by the constitution and laws of
the state of California.

The governing body of the authority shal1
consist of eleven (11) menbers conprised of the

Irvine Code Section VI .F-522.
You have asked whether, in light of the purposes and
composition of the authority, the effects on the econornic
interests of the nenbers would be considered the sane as the
effects on the public generally. Regulation 18703 provides that
under a narros set of circumstances the itpublic Aenerallyrl
exception nay be applied even nhere a significant segment of the
jurilaiction is not sinilarly affected. For exarnple, for_ local
elected officials, an industry, trade or profession of lrhich that
official is a menber nay constitute a significant segment of the
public Aenerally if that industry, trade or profession.ls a
predoninant industry, trade or profession ln the official's
jurisdiction or in the district rePresented by the official.
(Regulation 18703 (b) . )
Regulation 18703 also provides 3
(c) An industry, trade or profession
constitutes a significant segment of the public if
the statute, ordinance or other provj'sion of law
which creates or authorizes the creation of the
official's agency or office contains a finding and
declaration, including an exPress reference to
section 87103 of the Governnent Code, to the
following effect:
The Legislature [or other authority] declares
that the individualIs3 appointed to the office
of 
is [are] intended to represent
and-TurEtrEi-The interest of the [specified
industry, trade or professionl, and that such
representation and furtherance will ultiurately
serve the public interest. Accordingly, the
Legislature Ior other authority] finds that
for purposes of persons w.ho hold such office
the Ispecified industry, trade or profession]
is tantanount to and constitutes the public
generally within the neaning of Section 87103
of the covernnent Code.

(d) In absence of an express finding and
declaratLon of the type descrlbed in subsection (c)
of thls section, such an industry,' trade or Profes-
sion constitutes a significant segment of the
public generally only if such a finding and
declaration is implicit, taking lnto account the
langnrage of the statute, ordlnance or other provi-
sion of law creating or authorizing the creation of
the agency, the nature and purooses of the nroqran,
any applicable legislative hlstory, and any other
relevant circumstances.
Regulation 18703 (c) and (d),
emphasis added
The basis for Requlation 18703(c) and (d) is that under some
circunstances representatives of industries on regulatory boards
can vote pursuant to their own interest because their interests
are consistent with the goals of the board itself. 
The most
obvious exaruple of a regulatory board that falls within the scope
of the exception is a board created expressly to benefit a
specific industry, and the appointed Deluber is a representative of
the industry. In that situation, rrhen the nenber votes on a
matter which will affect his own industry, the official's private
interest is identical to the "pubJ.ic interest'r as defined by the
Iegislative purposes of the progran he is adninistering. (see
saiah caneron Hernorandun to the connission concerning the state
Board of Porestry. )
In re Callanan (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 33, provides - another
example. In the @!!ggE11 opinion, the Connission discussed
whether the nembers of the State Board of Funeral Directors and
E:nbalners could participate in decisions that would affect the
funera] industry. The Conrnission stated:
[w]hen the legislative body which creates a
reoulatorv board deterrnines that industry views and
expertise should be represented on the board, the
Political 
Reforrn Act should not be interpreted to
prevent industry menbers participating in board
decisions affecting the industry.
Emphasis added.
Moreover, in Consuner Union of U.S. Inc. v. California Milk
Producers Advisorv Board (L978) 82,CaI.App.3d 433, at p. 444, a

case which challenged the statutory authority for Regulation
18703, the court framed the guestLon in the case as follows:
[w]hether the PRA shou]d be read to nullify
the niny state and local lans establishing
regutatory boards and conni.ssions whose nenbers are
driwn fro:r the very industry, trade or profess.ion
regulated.
At the June sth Connission Ueeting, the Conmlssion was asked
to reconsider the appropriate application of Regulation 18703 to
resulatory boards. lccording to the minutes of that Cornrnission
,"6ting, Lhe connission deternined that Regulation 18703 (c) and
(d) should be applicable to neEbers of gry regulatory board,
wt ittrer created to benefit an industry, trade, or profession, or
to regulate the industry, trade or profession, provided the

The conmission decided to interpret regulation
18703 to Eean that an industry regulated by a board
is a significant segTment of the publlc generally
with relpect to decisions Dade or participated in
by industry Bembers of that board so long as.the
pirrpose of industry nenbership on the board is to
iurther and represent the interests of the
industry.

Comnission l,leeting,
page 3, iten 14.
Thus the exception has continued to be applied narrowly' For
exanple, in 1991, in the Strickland Advice Letter, No. I-91-046,
we slated that where a law mandates the inclusion of nenbers of

they represent.
l,toreover, even where a regulatory board falls within the
scope of Regulation 18703 (d) , we have cautioned that when a
decision wiIl have an unusual effect on the economic interests of
the public official, 
disqua l if ication rnay sti11 be required. For
example:
lAl nenber rrould be prohibited fron a
disciplinary proceeding against his establishment
or in the awarding of a research grant to hirnself

or his establishDent. Horever, pollcy decisions
whl,ch affect al.l or sone nenbers of the lndustry
should not trigger d lsqua J. if Lcat ion.
In re Callananr SEpEIL at P. 37.
[A] decision to increase the nunber of
recycling facilities rrill affect the entire
recycling facitity industry nhich constitutes the
upuLlic aenerallyrt for purposes of our discussion.
Thus, the recycling facillty operator which

nol be disqualified frorn partlcipating in this

Additionally, if a decision wiIl affect

Strickland Advice Letter, SUPEi!.
The Irvine Code does not inctude the express reference to
Section 87103 as set forth in subdivision (c) above. Thus we need
not address the effect of an express reference. However, we nust
still 
deternine whether it nay be inplied that the Eetnbers of the
authority are intended to represent and further the interests of
an induslry, trade or profession being regulated by the board, and
rrhether su-h representation rrill further the public interest.
Under your facts the transit authority was created to serve
the entire public, and not solelv to reoulate the industries in
ouestion. If an appointed menber of the authority votes
consistent with the interests of the association the menber
represents, there is no assurance that the vote wiII serve the
interests of the public. 
Moreover, the appointi,ng associations do
not appear to be industries, trades, or professions as the
regulation contemplates. with the exception of the Building
Industry Association of Southern california, aIl the nominating

conflicts of Interest
A public official is required to disclose afl his or her

decision in which the official knows or has reason to know the

Please note that participation in governmental decisions has
been interpreted broadly in furtherance of the goals of the Act.
Thus, trhere a member is disquali;iafi with respect to authority

Regulation 18703 (c) and (d),
emphasis added
The basis for Requlation 18703(c) and (d) is that under some
circunstances representatives of industries on regulatory boards
can vote pursuant to their own interest because their interests
are consistent with the goals of the board itself. 
The most
obvious exaruple of a regulatory board that falls within the scope

Political

prevent industry menbers participating in board
decisions affecting the industry.
